thickness_probe.interpretation: A Beer-Lambert win only means the 2 mm image predicts the 3 mm image better than copying 2 mm unchanged under this white-reference heuristic.

limitations: Delta-E is computed from robust center-crop median color, not radiometrically calibrated pixels.
The official images can depict different physical sheets and camera setups; labels establish material state, not pixel correspondence.
A four-delta-E nearest-state margin is an audit heuristic, not a learned classifier.
The affected-row count covers evaluated formulas only and is therefore a lower bound.
The thickness probe assumes the brightest image pixels approximate the light-table white reference and does not recover a camera response curve.
Nominal 2 mm/3 mm pairs are different physical sheets; only formula, gauge, and cold material state are shared.

rows.title: Marigold Yellow
rows.cold_characteristics: Consistent color.
rows.working_notes: Stable and consistent color. This style is not suitable for kilncasting because it can opalize and/or become incompatible when held at high temperatures for an extended period. It may also opalize and/or become incompatible in instances where processes exceed the parameters of the test for compatibility. Testing recommended when heatwork exceeds these parameters. Fusible / Bullseye-compatible.

rows.title: Lemon Lime Green
rows.cold_characteristics: Pale blue with yellow streaks.
rows.working_notes: Fires to an even, transparent green. This style is not suitable for kilncasting because it can become incompatible when held at high temperatures for an extended period. It may also become incompatible in instances where processes exceed the parameters of the test for compatibility. Testing is recommended when heatwork exceeds these parameters. Fusible / Bullseye-compatible.

rows.title: Gold Purple
rows.cold_characteristics: Appears as a dark transparent. May appear to be blue in color.
rows.working_notes: Color usually deepens on firing. Possible dark interface reaction with selenium and/or sulfur glasses (000137, 001122, 001125, 000124, 000125, 001137, 001437). Less viscous (softer) than most other glasses. Some gold-bearing striking glasses, like this one, should be fired with a 2 hour hold at 1225°F during the initial stages of the firing cycle. If fired without this hold, they may not strike at all, or they may strike but appear spotty and have a blue-brown cast, as opposed to the desired target color.

rows.title: Cranberry Pink
rows.cold_characteristics: Varies slightly from lighter to darker shade; sometimes with lighter dappling in single-rolled sheets.
rows.working_notes: Color usually deepens on firing. Possible dark interface reaction with selenium and/or sulfur glasses (000137, 001122, 001125, 000124, 000125, 001137, 001437). Less viscous (softer) than most other glasses. Some gold-bearing striking glasses, like this one, should be fired with a 2 hour hold at 1225°F during the initial stages of the firing cycle. If fired without this hold, they may not strike at all, or they may strike but appear spotty and have a blue-brown cast, as opposed to the desired target color. Know Your Gold Hold Fusible / Bullseye Compatible

rows.title: Salmon Pink Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Color range from paler to deep shades. Some variations from pink to brown tones. Slight mottling on back of sheet.
rows.working_notes: Generally deepens in hue on firing. Dark interface reaction possible with sulfur glasses (001137, 001437, 000137). Mottling disappears on firing. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Fuchsia
rows.cold_characteristics: Consistent color.
rows.working_notes: Color usually deepens on firing. Possible dark interface reaction with selenium and/or sulfur glasses (000137, 001122, 001125, 000124, 000125, 001137, 001437). Less viscous (softer) than most other glasses. Some striking glasses that use gold as their coloring agent, like this one, must be fired with a 2 hour hold in the range of 1200-1225°F during the initial stages of the firing cycle in its first firing to properly develop their color. If fired without this hold, or in a kiln that runs hotter than average, they may not strike at all, or they may strike but appear spotty and have a blue-brown cast, as opposed to their intended color. We have found that holding at 1200°F for 2:00 offers consistent color development with this style across a range of kilns and production dates, and functions as an adequate bubble squeeze for most projects. If your work depends on a bubble squeeze at 1225°F we recommend doing a small test on this style and other striking transparent pink glasses before beginning a project as they require precise heating to strike properly. For full information and testing strategies see the article Properly Pink, Purple and More .

rows.title: Gold Purple Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Appears a deep royal blue hue with areas of transparency and variations in color density.
rows.working_notes: Matures to purple upon firing, often with mottles and streaks remaining. Additionally, this style often fires with blue hues and is not uniform in color. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Plum Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Translucent purple with light plum wisps.
rows.working_notes: Strikes to a uniform color, often with small mottling. Expect variation across production dates. Color darkens with extended heat work. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Burnt Scarlet Tint
rows.cold_characteristics: Appears almost clear with blue/green tints.
rows.working_notes: Color usually deepens on firing. Possible dark interface reaction with selenium and/or sulfur glasses (000137, 001122, 001125, 000124, 000125, 001137, 001437). Less viscous (softer) than most other glasses. Some striking glasses that use gold as their coloring agent, like this one, must be fired with a 2 hour hold in the range of 1200-1225°F during the initial stages of the firing cycle in its first firing to properly develop their color. If fired without this hold, or in a kiln that runs hotter than average, they may not strike at all, or they may strike but appear spotty and have a blue-brown cast, as opposed to their intended color. We have found that holding at 1200°F for 2:00 offers consistent color development with this style across a range of kilns and production dates, and functions as an adequate bubble squeeze for most projects. If your work depends on a bubble squeeze at 1225°F we recommend doing a small test on this style and other striking transparent pink glasses before beginning a project as they require precise heating to strike properly. For full information and testing strategies see the article Properly Pink, Purple and More .

rows.title: Soft Yellow Opalescent, Deep Red
rows.cold_caption: Soft Yellow Opal, Deep Red, Streaky. Unfired
rows.fired_caption: Soft Yellow Opal, Deep Red, Streaky. Fired to 1490°F for :10 min.
rows.cold_characteristics: Originally developed for stained glass and mosaic applications. Expect color variation within a single sheet & throughout production runs. Hand-rolled and controlled for mix consistency, with the unique patterning of true art glass.
rows.working_notes: Constituent Glasses: Soft Yellow Opalescent (non-standard production) and Deep Red Transparent a.k.a. Copper Red (non-standard production) Each sheet contains a reactive combination that will continue to strike and develop with heatwork. Expect variation, which can occur within a single sheet & throughout production. 002250, like other Streaky sheet glasses, has natural variation in how the constituent glasses mix. Where sheets have more distinct areas of each constituent glass, they tend to maintain distinct coloration when fired. Where sheets have more layered and blended constituent glasses, they are prone to fire darker and more homogenous in color. The unfired composition of the sheet and the process temperature for firing both factor into the result. Hand-rolled and controlled for mix consistency, with the unique patterning of true art glass. Suitable for kilnforming, stained glass, and mosaics. Fusible / Bullseye-compatible.

rows.title: Garnet Red
rows.cold_characteristics: Appears light in color saturation with thin threads of color variation.
rows.working_notes: Matures to a more consistent color with deeper saturation. May contain subtle threads of darker color. This style is not suitable for kilncasting because it can opalize and/or become incompatible when held at high temperatures for an extended period. It may also opalize and/or become incompatible in instances where processes exceed the parameters of the test for compatibility. Testing recommended when heatwork exceeds these parameters.

rows.title: Cinnabar Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: May appear very muted gray/brown with streaks or variation in color density.
rows.working_notes: Matures to a red/cinnabar color. May appear slightly dappled. This style is not suitable for kilncasting because it can become incompatible when held at high temperatures for an extended period. It may also become incompatible in instances where processes exceed the parameters of the test for compatibility. Testing recommended when heatwork exceeds these parameters. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Butterscotch Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Opalescent. Slight small mottle and orange-peel texture.
rows.working_notes: At full fuse and capped with clear glass, the mottling usually remains. When fired uncapped, the mottling is less obvious and the color evens out to become more consistent. This style is not suitable for kilncasting because it can become incompatible when held at high temperatures for an extended period. It may also become incompatible in instances where processes exceed the parameters of the test for compatibility. Testing recommended when heatwork exceeds these parameters. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Carnelian
rows.cold_characteristics: Consistent color.
rows.working_notes: Stable and consistent color. This style is not suitable for kilncasting because it can opalize and/or become incompatible when held at high temperatures for an extended period. It may also opalize and/or become incompatible in instances where processes exceed the parameters of the test for compatibility. Testing recommended when heatwork exceeds these parameters. Fusible / Bullseye-compatible.

rows.title: Marigold Yellow Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: May appear partly transparent with wide variations in color density. Overall look of a lighter, brighter yellow such as Canary Yellow Opalescent (000120).
rows.working_notes: Matures to an opaque marigold yellow. May appear slightly dappled. This style is not suitable for kilncasting because it can become incompatible when held at high temperatures for an extended period. It may also become incompatible in instances where processes exceed the parameters of the test for compatibility. Testing recommended when heatwork exceeds these parameters. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Peach Blush Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Surface color variations common with color ranging from warm white to peach with variegated streaks. Underside of sheet can show patches of light and dark patterning from the forming process.
rows.working_notes: Matures to pastel peach upon firing, often with mottles and streaks remaining. Additionally, this style often fires with paler and darker hues and is not uniform in color. Underside of sheet can show patches of light and dark patterning from the forming process. Interface reaction possible with sulfur glasses, particularly 000137 French Vanilla Opal. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Purple Blush Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Appears a medium blue hue with variegated streaks of lighter and darker blue. Underside of sheet can show patches of light and dark patterning from the forming process.
rows.working_notes: Matures to pastel purple upon firing, often with mottles and streaks remaining. Additionally, this style often fires with blue and pink hues and is not uniform in color. Underside of sheet can show patches of light and dark patterning from the forming process. Interface reaction possible with sulfur glasses, particularly 000137 French Vanilla Opal. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Pink Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Surface color variations common; some light dappling. Color range from light pink to deeper shades of lavender pink. Slight mottling on back of sheet.
rows.working_notes: Typically deepens in coloration on firing. Dark interface reaction possible with sulfur glasses (001137, 001437, 000137). Mottling disappears on firing. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Pink Blush Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Surface color variations common with color ranging from cool white to pink with variegated streaks. Underside of sheet can show patches of light and dark patterning from the forming process.
rows.working_notes: Matures to pastel pink upon firing, often with mottles and streaks remaining. Additionally, this style often fires with a range of pale pink, darker pink and blue-ish pink hues and is not uniform in color. Underside of sheet can show patches of light and dark patterning from the forming process. Interface reaction possible with sulfur glasses, particularly 000137 French Vanilla Opal. Three production dates of lead bearing 000431-0030 horizontally as a base with sulfur bearing 000137 French Vanilla Frit in 03, 02, 01, 08 as vertical stripes on top surface showing the reactive potential. Sample was fired to a full fuse. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Pumpkin Orange Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: May contain variations in color density.
rows.working_notes: Matures to pumpkin orange, which may contain slight color variations. This style is not suitable for kilncasting because it can become incompatible when held at high temperatures for an extended period. It may also become incompatible in instances where processes exceed the parameters of the test for compatibility. Testing recommended when heatwork exceeds these parameters. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Spring Green Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Color variations common from minty-pastel to vibrant yellow-green.
rows.working_notes: Lighter coloration matures and becomes consistent to target upon firing. A sulfur glass. May react with lead and copper glasses to create dark interface (lead sulfide, copper sulfide). This style is not suitable for kilncasting because it can become incompatible when held at high temperatures for an extended period. It may also become incompatible in instances where processes exceed the parameters of the test for compatibility. Testing recommended when heatwork exceeds these parameters.

rows.title: Sunset Coral
rows.cold_characteristics: Color is transparent and varies in density.
rows.working_notes: Color usually deepens on firing. Possible dark interface reaction with selenium and/or sulfur glasses (000137, 001122, 001125, 000124, 000125, 001137, 001437). Less viscous (softer) than most other glasses. Some gold-bearing striking glasses, like this one, should be fired with a 2 hour hold at 1225ºF during the initial stages of the firing cycle. If fired without this hold, they may not strike at all, or they may strike but appear spotty and have a blue-brown cast, as opposed to the desired target color. Know Your Gold Hold Fusible / Bullseye Compatible

rows.title: Opaline Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Appears transparent and clear in its cold form. Occasional wisps of white are possible.
rows.working_notes: Requires a full fuse to strike to a hazy, milky white that transmits warm-hued light. Transmitted light varies based on thickness: Thinner pieces result in a yellow-ish hue, thicker pieces in a warmer almost orange hue. Opacity increases with extended heatwork. Opaline may remain clear or largely unstruck at temperatures in the tack fuse range (1275°F – 1425°F). To achieve target color and opacity when used in tack fuse projects a single sheet can be pre-struck at higher temperatures (1470/1480°F for :10). Expect the edges of the sheet to pull in from the original footprint and consider firing on 1/16” fiber paper topped with ThinFire for sheets larger than 10×10”. This allows air to escape laterally, thus preventing it from gathering between the sheet glass and kiln shelf in the form of a large bubble. The resulting struck sheet can then be cut and used in tack fused or other lower temperature works. Take note that the resulting struck sheet will have trace amounts of ThinFire/Shelf Primer on the shelf side, which can affect the results in secondary firings. Additionally, the opacity of the Opaline sheet may continue to develop in subsequent firings. Overlay Information Opaline has great potential to expand the color palette in kilnforming because it has the ability to create new colors with distinct properties. For more information, see Opaline Overlays . Helpful Resources Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Burnt Orange Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Opalescent. Slight small mottle and orange-peel texture.
rows.working_notes: At full fuse and capped with clear glass, the mottling may remain but become faint in transmitted light. Lighter wisps may appear in reflected light. When uncapped, the mottling tends to even out and the color becomes more consistent and even. This style is not suitable for kilncasting because it can become incompatible when held at high temperatures for an extended period. It may also become incompatible in instances where processes exceed the parameters of the test for compatibility. Testing recommended when heatwork exceeds these parameters. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Translucent White Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: May appear mostly clear with patches of thin milky white.
rows.working_notes: Opalizes upon firing. Consistent color, thin milky white. Color may become slightly streaky and transparent with excessive heatwork. Bullseye Compatible.

rows.title: Lacy White Opalescent
rows.cold_characteristics: Single Rolled -0000. White translucent glass with clear dapples, suggesting lace.
rows.working_notes: Designed for stained glass applications. Tested and graded for compatibility, but fusing will strike the sheet to solid white, with varying degrees of density. Light opalescent white. Cold sheet may show variation in opacity. Strikes to solid white when fused. Bullseye Compatible.
